Scope & Context Document
Version: 1.1
Date: 24 April 2026
Changes from v1.0:
Restructured around the six Zachman interrogatives (What, How, Where, Who, When, Why) per the Zachman Row 1 Planner research document
Rewritten to operate at executive/strategic level rather than product-feature level
Added explicit distinction between internal data model (ledger) and external Viewpoints
Row 6 detail relocated to future Row 6 Understanding exercise
Regulated-industry markets added as explicit strategic scope
Implementation-phasing "out of scope in v1" items removed — those are Row 3/4 decisions
21 v1.0 review comments resolved (see Appendix A for disposition)
About This Document
This document describes SysEngage at Zachman Row 1 (Planner / Contextual) level.
Per the Zachman Row 1 Planner research document (Zachman_Framework_Row_1_Planner.docx), Row 1 provides an executive, strategic view of the enterprise — its business purpose, scope, and context.
It does not address implementation, physical deployment, or detailed design; those belong to Rows 2 through 5.

The document follows the standard Zachman interrogative order (What, How, Where, Who, When, Why) with two framing sections:
Section 1 — Purpose: a concise mission statement orienting the reader
Sections 2–7 — the six Zachman interrogatives at Row 1 level
Section 8 — Row 1 Design Principles: cross-cutting strategic principles
Section 9 — Transition to Row 2
Section 10 — Strategic Risks
Section 11 — Glossary
Appendix A — Disposition of v1.0 review comments
Appendix B — Open Decisions

Content classes:
 [SOURCED] Content directly supported by Cluster A source material, the Zachman Row 1 Planner research doc, or the Row 1 Where column research paper.
[INFERRED] Content not explicitly stated but drawn as reasonable inference, with rationale.
[RESOLVED] Content where sources contradicted; one position adopted with rationale.
[OPEN] Content flagged for explicit decision.
[REVISED v1.1] Content newly written or substantially changed in v1.1 in response to v1.0 review comments.
1. Purpose
1.1 Mission Statement

[SOURCED] SysEngage is a tool that takes unstructured input describing a system or product, transforms it into a structured specification expressed across the Zachman Framework, and generates recommendations or information requests to fully define the described product.
Source: sys_engage_specification_v2.md line 3, with minor editing.
1.2 The Strategic Problem SysEngage Addresses
[INFERRED] Systems engineering practice requires turning natural-language descriptions of intended systems into structured, traceable, validated requirements.
This is historically labour-intensive, skill-sensitive, and produces inconsistent coverage across practitioners.
SysEngage exists to automate and systematise this transformation while preserving the rigour of the Zachman Framework and keeping the engineer in control of judgement and iteration.
1.3 Internal Data Model vs External Outputs (Critical Boundary)
[REVISED v1.1] Response to v1.0 comments C1 and C6.

SysEngage makes a fundamental distinction between what the tool stores internally and what the user sees:

 [INFERRED] This distinction matters at Row 1 because it bounds what is strategic-intent versus what is implementation-detail.
Row 1 commits to: (a) a structured internal data model exists, and (b) multiple user-facing Viewpoints are produced from it.
Rows 3–5 decide the physical realisation of both.
2. What (Data) at Row 1
[SOURCED] Per Zachman Row 1 Planner research: the What interrogative identifies the primary objectives, scope, and high-level data domains of the enterprise or system.
2.1 Strategic Objectives

[INFERRED] SysEngage exists to:
Reduce variability in the systems-engineering artefacts produced from unstructured input
Improve completeness of architectural coverage by surfacing gaps systematically rather than relying on reviewer memory
Provide a machine-readable canonical record of analyses, making systems-engineering work auditable
Lower the cost of systems-engineering rigour for organisations that cannot afford extensive specialist labour
Enable iterative refinement of requirements through human-in-the-loop dialogue
2.2 High-Level Data Domains
[REVISED v1.1] Per v1.0 comment C6: data domains at Row 1 are conceptual deliverable types, not ledger elements.
Ledger element listings belong at Row 3 (Designer) level.
The conceptual data domains SysEngage processes and produces:
2.3 What is Explicitly Out-of-Scope at Row 1 (Strategic)
[REVISED v1.1] Per v1.0 comments C7, C9, C10, C12, C14: the long list of implementation exclusions in v1.0 was wrong for Row 1.
Those belong at Row 3 (Designer) or Row 4 (Builder).
At Row 1, strategic out-of-scope items are much narrower.

At the strategic level, SysEngage does not:
Design the subject system itself.
SysEngage analyses and structures; the design of the system being described is the practitioner's responsibility (Row 1 spec v1.6 §1).
Replace the practitioner's judgement on iteration sufficiency.
SysEngage supports the decision of when a design is sufficiently complete to stop iterating, but does not make it autonomously (v1.0 comment C8).
Correct the practitioner's input.
Where input is contradictory or ambiguous, SysEngage surfaces the ambiguity rather than resolving it silently (Review Process v2.0 Non-Intervention Principle).
[INFERRED] All implementation-level choices (which ledger backend, what integrations, what platforms, code generation versus specification-only output) are deferred to Row 3/4/5 decisions.
They are not strategic exclusions at Row 1.
3. How (Function) at Row 1
[SOURCED] Per Zachman Row 1 Planner research: the How interrogative describes strategic methods and business rules at the high level.
Not business processes (Row 2) or logical design (Row 3).
3.1 Strategic Approach

[SOURCED] SysEngage's strategic method is governed AI-driven transformation of unstructured input into structured specifications, using the Zachman Framework as the internal classification taxonomy, with explicit human-in-the-loop refinement cycles.

Strategic methodology elements:
Iterative, not one-shot.
Quality improves through refinement cycles rather than first-pass completeness.

(v9.md Process Characteristics)
Governed transformation.
Every step declares its mode (Interpretive, Canonicalisation, Derivation, Literal Persistence).
Uncontrolled AI normalisation is prevented by design.

(SE-TMS-001)
Provenance-anchored.
Every derived artefact traces to verbatim source text.
Row context is established through shared provenance, not manual linking.

(Major Issues Q1)
Human-in-the-loop by design.
The practitioner is not supervising the tool, they are collaborating with it.
Phase 10 user review is first-class, not an afterthought.
(v9.md Phase 10)
3.2 High-Level Business Rules

[SOURCED] The following rules are strategic (they frame how SysEngage operates at all rows) and appear consistently across source material:
No source statement is silently discarded (Non-Loss)
Source text is preserved verbatim, byte-for-byte (Literal Persistence when required)
Every transformation declares an active mode (SE-TMS-001)
Ambiguity in input is preserved, not resolved arbitrarily
The same input produces the same output under the same model (Determinism)
The entire input is read (Whole-Document Coverage).
Partial processing is a defect.
Review of outputs is non-interventionist — observed issues are framed as process signals, not output corrections (Review Process v2.0 §4.1)
3.3 Iteration Sufficiency
[REVISED v1.1] Response to v1.0 comment C8: this was misframed in v1.0 as "not replace engineering judgement.
" The real Row 1 question is "when is iteration sufficient?

"
SysEngage supports, but does not autonomously make, the decision of when iterative refinement may terminate.
Strategic principle: iteration ends when the practitioner judges the specification is good enough for its intended downstream purpose, not when SysEngage detects zero remaining Gaps.
The definition of "good enough" is a practitioner-owned strategic judgement; the tool provides the evidence (coverage, gaps, quality scores) to inform it.
[OPEN] OD-R1-01: what evidence does SysEngage surface to support the iteration-sufficiency decision?
Candidate set: coverage percentage, unresolved-Gap count by severity, quality-score distribution, stakeholder-Question backlog.
Exact set to be resolved during Phase 2 process specification.
4. Where (Network) at Row 1
[SOURCED] Per Zachman Row 1 Where column research paper: Row 1 Where defines the enterprise's operational context — geographic regions, regulated industries, and organisational boundaries.
Explicitly excluded at Row 1: physical locations, technical environments, deployment platforms, network topologies (those are Row 3+ concerns).
4.1 Geographic Scope
[REVISED v1.1] Response to v1.0 comments.
Where was essentially absent in v1.0.
[INFERRED] SysEngage is strategically scoped for global operation.
It imposes no inherent geographic restrictions on the systems it analyses or on where it operates.
This is consistent with a tool designed to ingest natural-language input without region-specific assumptions.
[OPEN] OD-R1-02: confirm global-scope interpretation.
Alternative would be to scope for specific regional markets in v1 (e.g., UK/EU initially).
Global scope is the less-restrictive default.
4.2 Organisational Scope (Regulated Industries)
[REVISED v1.1] Response to v1.0 comment C5: compliance with regulated industries is in scope.
Standards discovery is a tool capability.
[SOURCED] SysEngage operates in regulated-industry markets.
Per the Where research paper, this aligns with TOGAF / FEAF / ISO-IEC-IEEE 15288 strategic-context definitions.

Regulated industries explicitly in strategic scope:
Healthcare (e.g., GDPR, HIPAA, MHRA, FDA, IEC 62304 and equivalents)
Defense (e.g., ITAR, DEF STAN, DO-178C, DO-254, MIL-STD-498)
Finance (e.g., SOX, Basel III, GDPR-financial, FCA conduct requirements)
Safety-critical (e.g., ISO 26262 automotive, IEC 61508 industrial, EN 50128 rail)
Any other regulated industry the user's input concerns

 [REVISED v1.1] Compliance-standards discovery is a tool capability.
SysEngage detects regulatory references in user input and identifies applicable standards as an analytical output, rather than hard-coding a fixed list of supported regimes.
This makes SysEngage extensible to new industries as input demands.
4.3 Enterprise Boundaries
[INFERRED] SysEngage operates within the enterprise scope defined by its user.
It does not assume a specific organisational setting (government, commercial, academic, hybrid).
It does assume the user has authority to supply input describing their intended system and to receive the analytical outputs.
4.4 What Row 1 Where is NOT

[SOURCED] Per the Where column research paper, Row 1 Where explicitly excludes:
Physical deployment locations (data centres, server rooms, cloud regions)
Technical environment choices (cloud versus on-premise versus edge)
Network topology (public cloud, VPN, air-gap)
Specific cloud providers or platforms

These are Row 3 (Designer) and Row 4 (Builder) concerns.
They are deferred intentionally at Row 1.
5. Who (People) at Row 1

[SOURCED] Per Zachman Row 1 Planner research: Who at Row 1 defines stakeholder groups and high-level roles at the strategic level, not detailed roles and responsibilities (Row 2) or user-interface specifics (Row 5).
5.1 Primary Stakeholder Groups
5.2 Secondary Stakeholder Groups
5.3 Stakeholder Groups Under Discussion
[OPEN] Response to v1.0 comments C15, C16: the following stakeholder groups were flagged for explicit discussion.
6. When (Time) at Row 1
[SOURCED] Per Zachman Row 1 Planner research: When at Row 1 defines high-level timelines and key milestones at the strategic level.
Not project schedules (Row 2) or runtime scheduling (Row 4).
6.1 Temporal Nature of SysEngage Use
[INFERRED] SysEngage is an iterative, session-spanning tool.

Temporal characteristics at the strategic level:
A single analysis typically spans multiple interaction sessions, not a single one-shot conversation.
State persists between sessions, carried by the canonical data model.
A complete analysis of a substantial system is measured in hours to days of practitioner effort, not minutes.
Analyses may be reopened long after initial completion to incorporate new inputs or observations from Row 6 operational feedback.
6.2 Iteration Termination (Strategic Criterion)
[REVISED v1.1] Response to v1.0 comment C8: iteration termination is a strategic When concern, not a Row 1 "non-purpose.

"
 [INFERRED] SysEngage iteration terminates when the practitioner judges "good enough for the intended downstream use.
" Good enough is not a fixed metric; it depends on what the Row N output will feed.
For early-stage concept exploration, high-level coverage may be sufficient.
For regulated-industry requirement handover to design, completeness and audit coverage become tighter.
For late-stage consolidation before implementation, essentially all gaps must be closed or explicitly accepted.
SysEngage provides the evidence (gap count by severity, coverage percentages, quality distributions) to support this decision.
Row 1 commits to "SysEngage informs iteration sufficiency" — the specific metric set lives at Row 2 (Owner) process decisions.
6.3 Temporal Scope of the Tool Itself
[INFERRED] SysEngage is intended to be used over a multi-year timeframe per installed instance.
Analyses and their source ledgers are long-lived records, not transient computations.

This implies:
Ledger schema versioning must be forward/backward compatible (this has already surfaced as risk RK-02)
Mode semantics must be stable across tool versions, or explicitly versioned
Stored analyses from a prior tool version must remain readable / processable by later versions
7. Why (Motivation) at Row 1
[SOURCED] Per Zachman Row 1 Planner research: Why at Row 1 explains the reasons and motivations behind the enterprise objectives and strategies.
Provides rationale and justification for the existence of the tool itself.
7.1 Rationale for SysEngage's Existence

[INFERRED] SysEngage exists because:
The gap between natural-language descriptions of intended systems and rigorous, structured, Zachman-framed specifications is large and persistent.
Practitioners producing specifications manually exhibit significant variance in coverage, rigour, and structural choices — even with the same input and the same framework.
Recent advances in AI language models make automated systematic transformation feasible in a way that was not practical a decade ago.
However, AI default behaviour (normalise, summarise, "improve") is counter to the systems-engineering requirement of preservation, rigour, and auditable provenance.
A governed methodology is needed.
An iterative human-in-the-loop approach, governed by explicit transformation modes, preserves practitioner control while amplifying practitioner output.
7.2 Strategic Benefits

[INFERRED] Expected benefits at Row 1 level:
Consistency — the same input, same model, same run produces the same outputs (Determinism principle)
Completeness — gaps are surfaced systematically rather than emerging during downstream rework
Auditability — every artefact traces to verbatim source text; every decision records its mode and rationale
Reduced practitioner variance — less skill-sensitive than manual Zachman analysis
Faster iteration — the cost of running the analysis again after input refinement is much lower than manual re-analysis
7.3 Strategic Justification at Enterprise Level
[INFERRED] Why would an enterprise adopt SysEngage?

Reduced rework cost in downstream design and build phases, because gaps are found earlier
Higher audit defensibility in regulated industries, because provenance is preserved
Lower talent barrier — practitioners need less Zachman-specific expertise to produce Zachman-quality output
Consistent artefact structure across projects, improving portfolio comparability and reviewability
7.4 Strategic Risks Justifying Caution
See Section 10 (Strategic Risks).
8. Row 1 Design Principles
These principles cross-cut all six interrogatives and govern behaviour at all rows.
They are Row 1 (strategic) content because they shape the tool's fundamental character, not its implementation details.
8.1 Non-Loss
[SOURCED] No source statement is silently discarded.
Every statement results in classification, traceability, gap capture, or explicit rejection with rationale.
(Row 1 spec v1.6 §2.2)
8.2 Verbatim Preservation
[SOURCED] Source text from the user's input is preserved exactly.
No paraphrasing, summarisation, or normalisation.
(Row 1 spec v1.6 §4.1; SE-TMS-001 Literal Persistence Mode)
8.3 Transformation Governance
[SOURCED] Every transformation declares an explicit mode.
Uncontrolled transformation is a defect.
This principle exists because AI default behaviour is to normalise content, which violates preservation.
(SE-TMS-001; Major Issues Q1)
8.4 Ambiguity Preservation
[SOURCED] Ambiguity in user input is preserved, not resolved arbitrarily.
If a statement has two possible interpretations, both are represented with appropriate confidence.

(Row 1 spec v1.6 §2.3)
[REFINED v1.2]  Per R1-AMEND-5 / D1 (Strand C) / R2-AMEND-1: this strategic commitment is operationalised at Row 2 by the Concern entity (CON-NNN), which captures ambiguous source content as a distinct lifecycle artefact requiring Practitioner clarification before classification proceeds.

The Concern entity is mutually exclusive with Signal at production time — a piece of source content produces either a Signal (clear, ready for classification) or a Concern (blocked on Practitioner clarification).
Row 1's strategic commitment is unchanged; Row 2 makes it operationally enforceable.
8.5 Derivation Lineage with Row-to-Row Signal Feed
[REVISED v1.1] Response to v1.0 comment C18: this principle was incomplete in v1.0.
Cross-row relationships use two mechanisms, not only one.
[REFINED v1.2]  Per R1-AMEND-6 / R2-AMEND-3: a third cross-row mechanism is added — independent source re-analysis at every row.
Top-down elaboration alone is insufficient; each row engages with the original source directly to surface what its analytical lens uniquely reveals.

[SOURCED] Cross-row relationships are established through:
Shared provenance — rows analysing the same original source material share provenance-based lineage (verbatim text matching, segmentation anchors).

(Major Issues Q1)
Upper-row signal feed — requirements produced at Row N act as additional Signals feeding analysis at Row N+1.

This means Row N+1 analyses the original source material AND the structured output of Row N.
Cross-row linkage is never established through manually authored hard links.
The system remains deterministic, re-analysable, and drift-detectable.
8.6 Process-Signal Framing
[SOURCED] When SysEngage behaviour is reviewed, observed issues are framed as process signals (the process could do better), not as defects requiring output correction.
Correction is out of scope for review; improvement is in scope.
(Review Process v2.0 §4.1)
8.7 Determinism
[SOURCED] Given the same input and model version, SysEngage produces the same output.
Non-determinism is either (a) bounded to semantically equivalent variants recognised as equivalent, or (b) a defect.
(Row 1 spec v1.6 §4.1; POC v5 Signal Verbatim Equivalence)
8.8 Whole-Document Coverage
[SOURCED] The entire input is read.
Partial processing is a defect.
Sampling is permissible only under an explicitly-invoked mode.
A Read Witness proves coverage.
(Row 1 spec v1.6 Pass 0 and Pass 0A)
8.9 Human-in-the-Loop By Design
[SOURCED] SysEngage is not fully autonomous.
User review is first-class.
Questions and Answers are first-class data-model elements.
(v9.md Process Characteristics).
[REFINED v1.2]  Per R1-AMEND-7 / D4 (Strand C) / R2-AMEND-7: human-in-the-loop is sharpened to require explicit Practitioner acts at row close.
Silence on closure decision is not permitted.

Strategic commitment: the Practitioner is not merely a supervisor whose absence implies consent — the Practitioner is the explicit authority whose acts close (or do not close) each row's analytical work.

At every row close, the Practitioner provides an explicit Closure Decision with one of three outcomes:
Accept — row closes; analytical work progresses to the next row
Reject — row close suspended; analytical work does not progress without escalation
Iterate — row close suspended; further iteration explicitly scoped
Why explicit acts matter: implicit consent (silence-equals-acceptance) treats the Practitioner as supervisory rather than authoritative.
The strategic commitment is that SysEngage trusts explicit Practitioner judgment, not implicit signals.
This is consistent with §3.3 Iteration Sufficiency (the Practitioner judges "good enough") and §8.6 Process-Signal Framing (the Practitioner, not the tool, decides corrective response).
The Closure Decision is the operational form of these strategic commitments at row boundaries.
Operationalisation: Row 2 v1.1 §8.15 Explicit Closure Principle and the ClosureDecision entity (CD-NNN) operationalise this strategic commitment.
Closure Decision is recorded as a first-class audit event at every row close, with diagnostics presented to the Practitioner before the decision is requested.
Per Strand C empirical evidence (R3-HOW-013 / R3-WHEN-010): without explicit closure discipline, rows can close on Gate verdict alone — bypassing Practitioner authority over project quality.
8.10 Actor Function Principle [NEW v1.2]
Statement: Actor roles in SysEngage are defined by the function they fulfil within the process, not by actor nature (human, AI, hybrid) or organisational position (internal, external).
The same role function may be fulfilled by different kinds of actors provided the actor's contribution conforms to the role's expected output.
SysEngage evaluates the contribution, not the contributor.
Strategic significance: this principle commits SysEngage to a function-based actor model rather than a nature-based or boundary-based one.

The strategic implication is that actor abstractions remain stable as the tool's deployment context evolves — for example, a mechanism-perspective role can be fulfilled by AI in v1, by a human expert in v2, or by a hybrid configuration in v3, without changing the role's process-level definition.
The architectural insight: stability lives in role functions; configurability lives in actor natures.
Why this principle now: during Strand C reconciliation, Strand A v2.14 proposed introducing an External Actor parent role using the internal/external axis.
The Practitioner identified that internal/external is the wrong axis of categorisation — the role-function axis is what matters.
This principle captures that insight at strategic level so future actor-model decisions at any row inherit the function-based discipline.
Strand A's External Actor proposal is explicitly NOT adopted.
Operationalisation: Row 2 v1.1 §8.14 Actor Function Principle.
SG-04 (Mechanism Stakeholder, renamed from "Internal Mechanism Stakeholders") gains an actor_nature attribute (Human/AI/Hybrid; v1 default = AI; reserved for future configurability).
Future versions may admit human or hybrid actors fulfilling SG-04's role function without changing the role definition.
(Source: Strand C D3 lock; Strand A R3-HOW-013 evidence about actor abstraction failure modes at Row 5; v1.2 R1-AMEND-1.)
8.11 Artefact-Backed Governance Principle [NEW v1.2]
Statement: Anything SysEngage commits to do reliably must leave an artefact behind.
Every normative commitment declared at one row is realised at downstream rows by a designed artefact and enforcement gate, or explicitly dispositioned at the relevant row with rationale.
Silent skipping of declared commitments is not permitted.
Strategic significance: this is a meta-principle about how SysEngage governs its own cross-row work.
The strategic commitment is that declared governance is insufficient — the tool's own governance must be artefact-backed, with realisation evidence persisted in the canonical record.
Without this principle, declared obligations become silent assumptions that may go unmet without detection.

Why this principle now: Strand A's empirical evidence (§12.1) demonstrated that R2-HOW-008 (mandatory source re-analysis) was declared at Row 2, not realised at Row 3, faithfully implemented at Row 4, and only caught by human review.
The principle Strand A derived from this failure: "Anything you want a system to do reliably must leave an artefact behind.
" The Practitioner accepted this as a Row 1 strategic commitment because it speaks to how SysEngage thinks about its own governance, not just how a particular obligation is enforced.

Relationship to other principles: this principle reinforces §8.1 Non-Loss (no source content silently discarded) and §8.2 Verbatim Preservation (no transformation silently mutates content) by extending the no-silence discipline to cross-row commitments themselves. §8.13 Schema Governance (below) is one specific instance of artefact-backed governance applied to the canonical schema.
Operationalisation: Row 2 v1.1 §8.16 Artefact-Backed Obligation Principle.
Row 3 inherits the Obligation Coverage Map deliverable per Strand C absorption A-2; closure gate logic at Row 3 Phase 9 blocks row closure if any upstream obligation lacks coverage or disposition.
The OCT (Obligation Coverage Trace) trace type backs the Map.
(Source: Strand C R2-AMEND-2; Strand A §12.1 empirical evidence; v1.2 R1-AMEND-2.
Strategic-name variant per Q1 lock — "Artefact-Backed Governance" reads more strategic than the operational "Artefact-Backed Obligation".)
8.12 Latent Construct Surfacing Principle [NEW v1.2]

Statement: SysEngage is permitted and expected to surface logical constructs implied by reference, influence, or lifecycle semantics in the source — even when the user did not articulate them explicitly.
Latent constructs are first-class analytical content; the absence of upstream articulation is not justification for silently omitting them.
Strategic significance: this principle commits SysEngage to active inference about implicit content, not just passive processing of explicit statements.
The strategic implication is that SysEngage's analytical depth is determined by what the source implies, not just what the source says.

Without this principle, SysEngage would be restricted to user-explicit content and would produce systematically incomplete analyses for any source where significant content is implicit (which is most natural-language inputs).
Why this principle now: Strand A v2.0 demonstrated the failure mode in the opposite direction — over-normalisation that lost source content.
Strand A v1.x demonstrated the failure mode of under-discovery — missing implicit content that the source clearly implied.
Strand A v2.4 surfaced four logical artefacts via Promotion Rules (Analysed System, Answer, Concern Category, Artefact State) that earlier versions had silently missed.

The Promotion Rules are operational mechanism; the strategic principle is that SysEngage commits to surfacing such constructs, with the discipline that the surfacing must itself be artefact-backed (per §8.11).
Relationship to other principles: this principle is distinct from §8.4 Ambiguity Preservation.
Ambiguity Preservation deals with multiple valid interpretations of explicit content.
Latent Construct Surfacing deals with implicit content that has no explicit form in the source but is implied by it.
Both reject premature resolution — Ambiguity Preservation rejects forced choice between alternatives, Latent Construct Surfacing rejects silent omission of implicit content.
Operationalisation: Row 2 v1.1 §8.18 Latent Discovery Principle.

Row 3 and downstream rows operationalise via Promotion Rules (PR-R3-01 Implied Reference Anchor; PR-R3-02 Behaviour-Influencing Input; PR-R3-03 Behaviourally Distinct Epistemic Differentiation; PR-R3-04 Logical State vs Baseline Separation).
Each surfaced construct gains a GT (Gap Trace) trace type recording that the construct was discovered through latent surfacing rather than upstream articulation.
(Source: Strand C R2-AMEND-4; Strand A §2.3 + PR-R3-01..04 evidence; v1.2 R1-AMEND-3.
Strategic-name variant per Q1 lock — "Latent Construct Surfacing" reads as active strategic commitment; Row 2's "Latent Discovery" is the operational form.)
8.13 Schema Governance Principle [NEW v1.2]
Statement: The canonical ledger schema is itself a first-class governed artefact within the SysEngage process.
The schema is not a passive data-model boundary but an actively-governed dependency.
Every row's output validates against the schema before closure; schema compliance is an explicit closure check, not an external responsibility.

Strategic significance: this principle clarifies a meta-level question about scope. §1.3 Internal Data Model vs External Outputs commits SysEngage to a structured internal data model existing — but did not say whether the schema defining that data model is inside or outside the process's governance scope. v1.2 commits to inside scope.
The schema is something the process binds to and validates against, not a passive definition that lives elsewhere.
Why this principle now: Strand A v2.13/v2.14 patch surfaced empirical evidence — schema treated as loose convention had drift between specification and ledger output.
Treating the schema as governed (inside-scope) enables the closure-time discipline that detects drift; treating it as ungoverned (outside-scope) leaves drift silent.
The Practitioner accepted this as a Row 1 strategic commitment because it answers a scope question ("is the schema governed by the process or external to it?
") that has implications for every row's closure discipline.
Relationship to §8.11: Schema Governance is a specific instance of Artefact-Backed Governance — schema compliance is one of the artefact-backed obligations that every row must discharge.
Stating this as a separate principle gives schema governance its own strategic visibility because the data-model boundary (§1.3) is itself a strategic concern.

Relationship to §1.3: this principle completes the §1.3 Internal Data Model boundary statement. §1.3 commits to a structured internal data model; §8.13 commits to the schema defining that data model being inside the process's governance scope.
Together they establish: there is a schema; the schema is governed; the schema's evolution is controlled within the process.

Operationalisation: Row 2 v1.1 §8.19 Schema Governance Principle and the Schema Compliance Check at Row 2 §3.6.6.1b. Every row's Phase 9 Readiness Gate runs the Schema Compliance Check against the canonical v2.10+v1.1 ledger schema; row close is hard-blocked on validation failures.
Row 3 produces a SchemaComplianceResult deliverable entity capturing the validation evidence.
(Source: Strand C R2-AMEND-5; Strand A §11 / R3-WHAT-017 / R3-HOW-017 / R3-WHEN-009 evidence about schema treated as loose convention with drift; v1.2 R1-AMEND-4.)
8.14 Principle architecture
The 13 v1.2 principles cluster into three governance areas, useful for navigation:
8.14.1Source-faithfulness principles (4)

§8.1 Non-Loss — no source content silently discarded
§8.2 Verbatim Preservation — source text preserved exactly
§8.4 Ambiguity Preservation — ambiguity preserved, not resolved arbitrarily
§8.8 Whole-Document Coverage — entire input read
18.4.2 Process-discipline principles (5)

§8.3 Transformation Governance — every transformation declares its mode
§8.5 Derivation Lineage with Row-to-Row Signal Feed [REFINED] — three cross-row mechanisms
§8.7 Determinism — same input + model → same output (semantic equivalence)
§8.10 Actor Function Principle [NEW] — roles defined by function, not nature
§8.12 Latent Construct Surfacing Principle [NEW] — implicit content actively surfaced
18.4.3 Authority and governance principles (4)

§8.6 Process-Signal Framing — observed issues are process signals, not output corrections
§8.9 Human-in-the-Loop By Design [REFINED] — explicit Practitioner acts at closure
§8.11 Artefact-Backed Governance Principle [NEW] — committed work leaves artefacts
§8.13 Schema Governance Principle [NEW] — schema is governed, validated, in-scope
This grouping is informational only — the canonical numbering (§8.1-§8.13 in current order) is preserved.
9. Transition to Row 2 (Owner)
[SOURCED] Per Zachman Row 1 Planner research: Row 1 establishes the strategic foundation; Row 2 (Owner) translates that foundation into detailed business processes and operational models.
This document does not define Row 2 content — that is the next document in the Row-by-Row Understanding series.

What Row 1 hands to Row 2:
A mission statement and strategic objectives
A high-level data-domain map
A strategic-method commitment (governed AI transformation, human-in-the-loop, Zachman-internal)
Geographic and regulatory scope commitments
Stakeholder-group identifications
Temporal expectations (multi-session iteration, multi-year tool life)
Motivational justifications for the enterprise-level use
Cross-cutting design principles that bound Row 2 and beyond

 [REVISED v1.1] Response to v1.0 comment C19: Row 6 detail has been removed from Row 1 Understanding.
Row 6 (Operating Enterprise) will be addressed in its own Row 6 Understanding document when that work is undertaken.
That is the correct place to reason about operational telemetry, deployment feedback loops, and runtime reality checks.
10. Strategic Risks

Risks to SysEngage at the strategic level (not risks to systems SysEngage analyses):
[REVISED v1.2]  Two new strategic risks added (RK-09, RK-10) propagated from the Row 2 v1.0 → v1.1 amendment cycle.
Three existing risks (RK-01, RK-02, RK-06) gain refined mitigation notes reflecting v1.1 Row 2 mechanisms.
Five existing risks (RK-03, RK-04, RK-05, RK-07, RK-08) preserved verbatim.
10.1 Strategic Risk Register
10.2 v1.2 Risk Commentary
10.2.1 Refinements (RK-01, RK-02, RK-06)
RK-01 (AI model drift): v1.2 adds the ai_model_fingerprints audit foundation as the new mitigation mechanism.

Previously, the strategic mitigation rested on SE-TMS-001 mode discipline (preventing inappropriate normalisation) and the Literal Persistence Mode override. v1.1 Row 2 added a complementary detection mechanism — model identity recorded per execution.
The strategic effect: drift is no longer just prevented (by mode discipline); it is also detectable (by fingerprint comparison).
RK-02 (Schema evolution drift): v1.2 adds the Schema Governance Principle (new §8.13) as a strategic discipline complementing Canonicalisation Mode.
Previously, schema migration was handled by Canonicalisation Mode for explicit migration tasks; this addressed the migration event but not ongoing schema-vs-output drift between migrations.
The Schema Governance Principle adds continuous discipline — every row's output validates against the schema at row close, so drift is detected when it happens rather than at the next migration.
RK-06 (AI-vendor concentration): v1.2 adds ai_model_fingerprints as supporting evidence for the model-agnostic strategic stance.

Previously, the model-agnostic stance was a process commitment ("specs are model-agnostic") with no audit trail enabling cross-vendor verification. v1.1 captures actual model identity per execution, making the audit trail itself portable across vendors.
The strategic effect: the model-agnostic claim is now empirically verifiable, not just declared.
10.2.2 New risks (RK-09, RK-10)
RK-09 (Practitioner workload from explicit-decision discipline): Surfaces a strategic-level cost of the §8.9 Human-in-the-Loop refinement.

SysEngage's strategic commitment to explicit Practitioner authority — sharpened in v1.2 to require explicit Closure Decisions at every row, explicit Concern dispositions before row close, explicit Override rationales when Gate verdicts are overridden — creates a Practitioner-side workload that scales with project complexity.
This was identified during the Row 2 v1.1 amendment cycle as the operational risk OR-12; the strategic-level counterpart at Row 1 captures it as a strategic concern about adoption barrier.
Important: the principle (§8.9) is unchanged — what changes is visibility of its cumulative cost.
The four mitigation paths in the register (Suggestion-accompanies-Question, threshold tuning, fast-path dispositions, future UX) preserve the principle while reducing burden.

RK-10 (AI ensemble determinism boundary): Surfaces a forward-strategic risk visible from the OD-R2-27 resolution path. v1's commitment to ensemble-of-size-1 schema (with semantic-equivalence determinism) is a defensible v1 commitment but does not yet reach the byte-determinism that some regulated profiles may require.

The risk is not a v1 defect — it is a known boundary of v1's commitments, with v2 enhancement candidates queued (model pin/float, ensemble operational shape, byte-determinism modes for regulated profiles).
Strategic significance: this risk is forward-looking.
It says "here is what v1 does not yet support, and here is the design pressure for v2.
" Adoption in safety-critical domains may be constrained until v2 work is undertaken; the risk register makes this constraint visible at strategic level.
10.2.3 Risk register integrity
v1.2 risk register is consistent with v1.2's strategic principle additions.
Every new principle (§8.10-§8.13) and every refinement (§8.4, §8.5, §8.9) has been examined for risk implications.
New risks identified at strategic level (RK-09 from §8.9 refinement; RK-10 from §8.7 alignment context with OD-R2-27 deferred items) appear in the register.
Mitigation refinements (RK-01, RK-02, RK-06) reflect the v1.1 Row 2 mechanisms now available to support strategic-level mitigation.
The Artefact-Backed Governance Principle (§8.11) applied to the risk register itself: declared strategic mechanisms produce identifiable failure modes, which are catalogued and mitigated.
11. Glossary (Row 1 minimum)
[REVISED v1.2]  §11 v1.2 retains all 9 v1.1 entries verbatim and adds 6 strategic-level entries reflecting v1.2 principle additions and refinements.
Each new entry captures the term's strategic meaning at Row 1 — operational definitions belong at Row 2 §11.
[INFERRED] Each v1.2 entry uses Row 1 strategic-meaning phrasing — what the term means at strategic level for SysEngage.
Operational mechanics (ClosureDecision entity attributes, RowScopedSourceFindings entity, Schema Compliance Check semantics, etc.) are defined in Row 2 §11 Glossary.
The two glossaries are complementary — Row 1 captures strategic intent; Row 2 captures operational form.
Appendix A. Disposition of v1.0 Review Comments
Each of the 21 review comments raised against v1.0 is addressed below with the action taken in v1.1.
Appendix B. Open Decisions
Open decisions remaining after the v1.0→v1.1 revision.
All are genuinely Row 1 (strategic) decisions, not implementation-phasing.

Open Decisions removed between v1.0 and v1.1:
OD-01 (deployment model), OD-02 (user persona), OD-03 (input scope), OD-05 (ledger persistence), OD-06 (commercial positioning), OD-07 (regulated-domain handling), OD-08 (AI dependency), OD-09 (determinism expectation), OD-10 (Row 6 depth) — all either incorporated into the interrogative sections as Row 1 strategic content, or removed as implementation-phasing decisions that belong at Row 3/4.
